agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive 2025 Homeless Count report for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council received the 2025 Homeless Count report, which identified 2,715 homeless individuals on March 11, 2025 (1,952 sheltered, 763 unsheltered), a 12% increase from 2023. 79% of respondents reported being homeless for at least a year. 42% of respondents identified as Indigenous. The report is for information only; no vote or bylaw was proposed.

(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed 2026 Cultural Grants totaling $5,509,770 for 85 organizations. Grants cover operating costs (Institutions: $2,828,160; Annual: $709,760; Multi-year: $1,897,550/year for 2026-2028) and cultural spaces (including $75,000 additional to Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society, and $728,000 to Historic Joy Kogawa House Society for hazardous materials abatement and accessibility upgrades). Recommendations A-C, E, and G require a 2/3 supermajority vote under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — if approved, this could affect funding for your arts or cultural organization.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to award 19 grants totalling $89,000 to community sport events in Vancouver during Spring 2026. The grants range from $1,000 to $5,000 per event, with events scheduled from May 1 to August 30, 2026. Approval requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a community sport event in Vancouver, watch for the council decision — this could mean funding opportunities for your Spring 2026 event.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a recommendation to set the 2026 general purpose tax share at approximately 57.5% residential and 42.5% non-residential, with no tax shift proposed. The total general purposes tax levy is $1,224,250,000, based on a 0% tax increase approved in the 2026 Operating Budget. Tax rates will be adjusted for assessment market changes to ensure revenue neutrality.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting on April 14, 2026, and submit comments to Council before the meeting to have your say on the proposed tax distribution.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 2026 property tax distribution

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council received the 2025 Property Tax Exemptions report. In 2025, $60.9 billion of properties (12% of the assessment roll) were exempt, resulting in $335.1 million in forgone general purpose taxes, partially offset by $34.8 million in compensating payments-in-lieu of taxes (PILTs). Any net forgone taxes are redistributed to other taxpayers. Statutory exemptions apply automatically to Crown lands, City property, charitable institutions, schools, hospitals, churches, emergency shelters, and others. Permissive exemptions are discretionary by Council by-law.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy. The policy clarifies the City's role and establishes that City-built structural flood measures (e.g., dikes, tide gates) outside of redevelopment will only be considered in specific situations to preserve City facilities and core services, subject to financial capacity. It leverages land-use authority and redevelopment to incrementally improve flood resilience, formalizing existing practices.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in a proposed flood-risk zone and review the draft policy before the council vote.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on public plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposal to designate 13 public plazas for alcohol consumption from 2026 to 2029, plus a 2-year extension for the Drinker's Lounge parklet at 111 Princess St. The program transitions to a 3-year cycle. Permissible hours align with nearby washroom operating hours. All proposed sites have washroom access via portable units or signage to nearby facilities. The program has operated without incident since 2022.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 14, 2026 council meeting and attend or submit comments if your business is near a proposed plaza site.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to apply for development approvals funding
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on applying for a $200,000 provincial grant through the Local Government Development Approvals Program. If approved, the funding would be used to redesign approval processes for large-scale housing developments, including creating reusable guides, templates, and service blueprints to simplify regulations and reduce delays. The grant would cover 100% of eligible project costs.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could speed up your future development applications in Vancouver.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing rezoning condition changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider 11 housing rezoning condition changes for specific Vancouver sites, including adjustments under the Rental Development Relief Program (RDRP). The RDRP is a two-year pilot (Feb 1, 2026 – Dec 15, 2027) for eligible mid- and high-rise below-market rental projects. Eligible projects may adjust affordability from a 20% discount to CMHC city-wide average rents to rates not exceeding city-wide averages for all purpose-built rentals. Projects seeking only a reduction in affordability (Stream 1) must enter a new or amended Housing Agreement subject to further Council approval.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you have a rezoning application in progress for one of the 11 listed sites, review the RDRP eligibility criteria and contact your city councillor before the April 14, 2026 meeting.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) Bylaw No. 13493 for properties at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The decision has not been made yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning at 4088 Granville Street, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone 4088 Granville Street from FSD (First Shaughnessy) District to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. The proposal permits three three-storey residential rental buildings with 31 units, a maximum floor space ratio (FSR) of 1.2, and a maximum building height of 13.4 m (44 ft.). At least 35% of units must have 2+ bedrooms. The site is not a protected heritage property.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Ave and 2553 Highbury St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning from RR-3B to CD-1 for 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. If approved, the project would allow a six-storey mixed-use building with 82 secured market rental units and commercial at grade (minimum 594.9 m²). A time-limited condition requires that if a Stage 1 building permit is not issued within two years of the rezoning approval, at least 20% of residential floor area must revert to below-market rental units. The maximum floor space ratio is 3.50, building height is 22 m, and at least 35% of units must have 2+ bedrooms. The Sign By-law and Noise Control By-law would also be amended.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby amenity cost charge bylaw and density bonus updates
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider updates to how the city collects money from new developments to pay for community amenities like parks, community centres, and childcare facilities. A proposed Amenity Cost Charge bylaw would set standard fees for developers, replacing the current system of negotiating each project individually. Council will also consider updates to the Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning policies, which allow developers to build more units in exchange for providing affordable housing or cash contributions. These changes are part of the city's 2026 Financing Growth Update. A public consultation process is required before any bylaw is adopted.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for public hearing dates — developers and property owners should prepare to provide input on the proposed bylaw.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess Trounce Alley & Blood Alley Square maintenance costs
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. This is a 'local improvement' project, meaning the city pays for the work upfront and then recovers the cost from the property owners who benefit directly. If approved, the bylaw would add a special assessment to the property taxes of affected landowners to cover things like cleaning, repairs, and upkeep for the year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley Square assessment area — contact Burnaby's engineering department to confirm.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would assess (charge) property owners for the 2025 costs of the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project. This means the City would collect money from specific property owners to help pay for shared parking in that area. agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in certain conditions, like how many affordable rental units must be provided and for how long. This proposal has not been approved yet — Council will consider it at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw that would amend an existing Housing Agreement for three properties: 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules about how the property can be used — often to keep some units affordable. This proposed change would update the terms of that agreement. The decision has not been made yet.

agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend the existing CD-1 (198) zoning rules for the properties at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue. This amendment would change what is allowed to be built or done on that site. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on it at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue. The proposal would change the land-use designation to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone, increasing the maximum floor space ratio from 3.0 to 8.1 and the maximum building height from 9.2 m (30 ft.) to 50.6 m (166 ft.) to permit a 16-storey commercial building with 152 hotel rooms and ground-floor commercial space. No public hearing is required because the proposal is consistent with the Vancouver Official Development Plan and the Broadway Plan.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Fast-track rental housing and small hotels in new Burnaby mixed-use district
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposal to create a new 'Mixed-Use Commercial District' that would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels. If approved, this proposed bylaw would allow developers to build rental apartments and small hotels (under a certain size) more quickly in specific commercial zones, without needing a full rezoning process each time. The goal is to speed up the construction of much-needed rental homes and tourist accommodations. Council will consider the referral report at an upcoming meeting before any final decision is made.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to comment on the proposed bylaw before Council votes.

minutes_briefs.title: Council received 2025 Homeless Count report, calls on senior governments for action
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council received the 2025 Homeless Count report for information and voted unanimously to call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to take several actions. Council is asking for increased investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence that contributes to homelessness. They also want the senior governments to re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre, expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and recovery services across Metro Vancouver and BC, strengthen discharge planning from health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent people from being released into homelessness, and provide support to local governments for daytime sheltering and housing with supports.
minutes_briefs.industries: Healthcare, Labour & Employment, Landlord & Property Mgmt
minutes_briefs.source_body:
1.
2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
April 1, 2026

The General Manager, Arts, Culture and Community Services along with staff from Arts, Culture
and Community Services, provided a presentation and responded to questions.

MOVED by Councillor Dominato
SECONDED by Councillor Klassen

A.
THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless
Count – Vancouver” for information.

B.
THAT Council call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to:  

a.
Increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports,
and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to
homelessness;

b.
Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre;  

c.
Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and
recovery services across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout
British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to
ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access
close to home and community;  

d.
Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care,
corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being
discharged into homelessness; and  

e.
Provide support to Local Governments for daytime sheltering.  

amended


AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Fry
SECONDED by Councillor Maloney

THAT the words “and housing with supports” be added to the end of e.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11512)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having carried, the motion as amended was put and CARRIED
UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11513) with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

FINAL MOTION AS APPROVED

A.
THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless
Count – Vancouver” for information.

B.
THAT Council call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to:  

a.
Increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports,
and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to
homelessness;

b.
Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre;  

c.
Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and
recovery services across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout
British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to
ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access
close to home and community;  

d.
Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care,
corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being
discharged into homelessness; and  

e.
Provide support to Local Governments for daytime sheltering and housing
with supports.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $9.3M in cultural grants, plus $25,000 for Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a total of $9.3 million in cultural grants for 2026. This includes operating grants for five institutions ($2.8 million), annual grants for 26 organizations ($709,760), and multi-year grants for 52 groups ($1.9 million per year for three years). Council also added an extra $25,000 from reserves for the Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden Society. Additionally, Council approved a $728,000 one-time grant to the Historic Joy Kogawa House Society for hazardous materials removal and accessibility upgrades, and $250,000 in total to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society for a feasibility study. All decisions were final and passed unanimously.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required. Eligible organizations should prepare to sign grant agreements with the City.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 19 community sport event grants totalling $89,000
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved 19 community sport event grants worth a total of $89,000. The money comes from the 2026 Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program Budget. Each organization listed in the report will receive the amount shown beside its name, but only after signing a grant agreement letter. Council also declared that any organization that is not a registered charity is still considered to contribute to the city's culture, beautification, health, or welfare, which allows them to receive the grant.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
3.
Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
March 10, 2026

A.
THAT Council approve 19 new Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants
totalling $89,000 as outlined in the body of the Report dated March 10, 2026,
entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” to
each organization listed in Table 1 of the same report, under the column entitled
“Organization” and for the amount set out beside their name in the column
entitled “Grant Recommended”; source of funding is the 2026 Vancouver
Community Sport Hosting Program Budget.

B.
THAT the grants in A above be subject to each grant recipient agreeing to the
terms and conditions of the community sport hosting grant program as generally
outlined in this report and otherwise satisfactory to the Director, Tourism and
Destination Events and the City Solicitor.  

C.
THAT no legal rights or obligations are created by the approval of the A and B
above unless and until the grant agreement letter is executed and delivered by
the grant recipient.  

D.
THAT pursuant to the Section 206(1)(i) of the Vancouver Charter, the
organizations listed in Table 1 of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled
“Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” which are not
otherwise a registered charity with Canada Revenue Agency are deemed by
Council to be an organization contributing to the culture, beautification, health, or
welfare of the City.  

ADOPTED ON CONSENT AND A  
BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11523)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Council adopted Coastal Flood Management Policy, rejected task force amendment
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has adopted the Coastal Flood Management Policy, which outlines the City's role in planning and managing both structural (like seawalls) and non-structural (like land-use rules) measures to protect against coastal flooding and sea level rise. A proposed amendment to create an intergovernmental task force for the False Creek floodplain was defeated. The policy is now in effect.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics
minutes_briefs.source_body:
6.
Coastal Flood Management Policy
March 17, 2026

Staff from Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability and Legal Services responded to
questions.

MOVED by Councillor Maloney
SECONDED by Councillor Meiszner

THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in
Appendix A of the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management
Policy,” which articulates the City’s role and supports planning, implementation, and
management of structural and non-structural coastal flood management measures in
Vancouver.

carried


AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Maloney
SECONDED by Councillor Orr

A.
THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as
presented in Appendix A to the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal
Flood Management Policy.”

B.  
THAT Council directs staff to report back on the scope of work, governance
options, resource requirements, and timeline necessary for the City to take the
steps necessary to convene, or cause to be convened, an intergovernmental and
inter-agency task force for the False Creek floodplain, for the purpose of:

1.
identifying major publicly owned, utility, transportation, health, and civic
assets at risk from coastal flooding and sea level rise;

2.
assessing whether a coordinated, area-wide structural flood management
approach would be more effective and cost-efficient than fragmented site-
by-site measures;

3.
identifying the appropriate roles of the City, Province, Government of
Canada, First Nations, agencies, utilities, and other major landowners
and infrastructure owners, including, but not limited to, Canada Mortgage
and Housing Corporation in respect of Granville Island, the Department of
National Defence and/or the Canadian Coast Guard in respect of the
Kitsilano Coast Guard Station, Fisheries and Oceans Canada in respect
of the False Creek Harbour Authority, PAVCo in respect of B.C. Place,
Providence Health Care in respect to St. Paul's Hospital, Vancouver
Fraser Port Authority, Vancouver Airport Authority, TransLink, BC Hydro,
Creative Energy, and such other affected public or regulated entities as
staff may determine;

4.
identifying advocacy, research, technical, and partnership work required
to support any future funding strategy or senior-government engagement;
and

5.         reporting back on options for a fiscally prudent convening and
coordination role for the City that does not prejudice future Council
decisions on capital commitments.

C.  
THAT the report back in B above also assesses whether similar
multi-jurisdictional coordination challenges exist in the Burrard Inlet and Fraser
River floodplains, or whether differences in land ownership, infrastructure
ownership, and distribution of benefits mean that a False Creek-specific
approach is warranted.

LOST (Vote No. 11516)
(Councillors Dominato, Klassen, Meiszner, Montague, Zhou and Mayor Sim opposed)
(Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having lost, the motion was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No.
11517) with Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved in principle public plaza alcohol consumption program through 2028
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted unanimously to approve, in principle, changes to the City Land Regulation By-law that will allow alcohol consumption in certain public plazas. The program will run until 2028. Council directed the Director of Legal Services to prepare a final by-law that officially designates the specific plaza locations listed in the March 17, 2026 staff report. One speaker opposed the plan during the meeting, but council moved forward with the decision.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required — the by-law amendment is still being drafted; watch for the final by-law enactment and effective date.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning condition amendments for multiple Burnaby housing developments
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted unanimously to approve amendments to housing rezoning conditions for several developments, as outlined in Appendix A of a staff report. The changes will be integrated into the respective Housing Agreements for each development, but only if the property owner agrees. Council also instructed the Director of Legal Services to prepare the necessary by-laws to make these changes official. This decision is final and creates no legal rights or obligations for the City until the by-laws are enacted.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are a property owner listed in Appendix A of the staff report, contact the Planning Department to review and agree to the amended conditions before they are integrated into your Housing Agreement.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rental housing text amendment at 5590 Victoria Drive
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved a text amendment to the CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The change increases the floor space ratio (the total building size allowed on the property) from 3.49 to 3.5 and switches the housing from for-profit affordable rental (leased by a non-profit) to secured market rental housing. The developer must still sign a Housing Agreement bylaw before the new rules take effect.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 rezoning for 31 rental units at 4088 Granville Street
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a rezoning application for 4088 Granville Street, changing the land from First Shaughnessy (FSD) District to Comprehensive Development (CD-1) District. This allows the owner to build three three-storey residential rental buildings with 31 units. The approval increases the maximum floor space ratio (FSR) — the ratio of a building's total floor area to the size of the land it sits on — from 0.5 to 1.2, and raises the maximum building height from 11.5 metres (38 feet) to 13.4 metres (44 feet). The decision is subject to conditions, including a Housing Agreement that must be finalized before the new zoning bylaw takes effect.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 rezoning for 6-storey mixed-use building at 3803 West 10th
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved a rezoning application for 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. The property will be rezoned from RR-3B (Residential Rental) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. This allows a six-storey mixed-use building with commercial space on the ground floor and 82 secured market rental housing units. If a stage one Building Permit is not issued within two years of this approval, the developer must include below-market rental units. Council also approved the necessary changes to the Sign By-law and Noise Control By-law to match the new zoning.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are the property owner or developer, ensure you apply for a stage one Building Permit within two years to avoid the requirement to add below-market units.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 20-storey rental tower at 310 East 14th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a rezoning application for 310 East 14th Avenue, allowing a 20-storey rental apartment building. The property was rezoned from R3-3 (Residential) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District, which is a special zone for this specific project. The new rules allow a floor space ratio (FSR) of 6.53 — meaning the building's total floor area can be 6.53 times the size of the lot — up from 2.70. The maximum building height increased from 23 metres (75 feet) to 60 metres (197 feet). At least 20% of the residential floor area must be set aside as below-market rental units, meaning they will be rented at rates lower than typical market prices. The approval is conditional on the owner signing a Housing Agreement with the city to secure those affordable units.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or manage rental properties in Burnaby, review how the new below-market rental requirements at this site may affect local rental market conditions.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved consultation on new Burnaby Amenity Cost Charge By-law
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to direct staff to consult with the public and development industry stakeholders on a proposed Amenity Cost Charge By-law and updates to the Zoning and Development By-law related to Bill 16. This means the city will now gather feedback from developers, the Urban Development Institute (UDI), NAIOP, and the Homebuilders Association Vancouver (HAVN) before finalizing the new rules. The decision was adopted on consent, with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are a developer or property owner in Burnaby, review the proposed Amenity Cost Charge By-law and Bill 16 updates and prepare to provide feedback during the upcoming consultation period.
minutes_briefs.source_body:

2026 Financing Growth Update – Required Consultation for Amenity Cost Charge
By-law and Bill 16 Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning Updates
March 31, 2026

consultation, direct staff to consult on the proposed Amenity Cost Charge By-law and
Bill 16 Updates to the Zoning and Development By-law with:

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved bylaw to assess Trounce Alley properties for 2025 maintenance
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously passed a bylaw (By-law No. 14670) to assess property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for 2025 maintenance costs. This is a Local Improvement Project, meaning the costs for upkeep are charged directly to the properties that benefit from the alley and square. The bylaw is now in effect, and property owners in that area will see the assessment on their property tax bill.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 rezoning for 16-storey hotel at 888 West 8th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved a rezoning application for 888 West 8th Avenue, changing the land from a C-3A (Commercial) district to a CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) district. This allows the owner to build a 16-storey commercial building with 152 hotel rooms and ground-floor retail space. The approval increases the maximum floor space ratio (the amount of floor area allowed relative to the lot size) from 3.0 to 8.1, and the maximum building height from 9.2 metres (30 feet) to 50.6 metres (166 feet). The decision was unanimous, with two councillors absent.

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council approved new C-2A zone to fast-track rental housing and small hotels
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to create a new mixed-use commercial zone called C-2A. This new zoning is designed to fast-track the development of rental housing and small hotels. Council approved changes to several bylaws — including the Zoning and Development By-law, the Rental Housing Stock Official Development Plan, the Subdivision By-law, the Sign By-law, the Noise Control By-law, the Parking By-law, and the Downtown Eastside/Oppenheimer Official Development Plan — to make the new C-2A zone possible. The decision was made on March 3, 2026, and the matter has been referred to a public hearing for final input before the new rules take effect.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in an area being rezoned to C-2A and prepare to attend the public hearing to understand how this affects your business.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 42.5% non-residential property tax share for 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved the 2026 property tax distribution, setting the non-residential share at approximately 42.5% and the residential share at approximately 57.5%. This means businesses and commercial property owners will pay a slightly lower proportion of the total property tax levy compared to previous years. The Director of Finance has been instructed to calculate the specific tax rates for all property classes to achieve this split. This decision is final and will affect your 2026 property tax bill.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review your 2026 property tax assessment and budget for the new non-residential tax rate now that the share has been finalized.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services

THAT Council instruct the Director of Finance to calculate the 2026 general purpose tax

and approximately 42.5% non-residential.

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council adopted $2.39B 2026 budget with tax levy
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has adopted the 2026 budget, approving $2.39 billion in total gross expenditures. The budget includes a General Purposes Tax Levy of $1.22 billion, which is the amount that will be raised through property taxes to fund city services. The budget was adopted on consent, meaning it passed without debate, with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
4a.
Budget Resolution

THAT WHEREAS section 219 of the Vancouver Charter requires that the Director of
Finance submit to Council each year the detailed estimates of revenues and
expenditures of the City for the year;

AND WHEREAS section 372 of the Vancouver Charter requires that Council adopt the
estimates of revenues and expenditures for the City as soon thereafter as possible after
considering the report submitted under section 219 and receiving the revised
assessment roll;

AND WHEREAS the total estimated gross expenditures of the City to pay all debts and
obligations of the City falling due in the year 2026, exclusive of the amounts required for
school, Greater Vancouver Regional District, Greater Vancouver Transportation
Authority, British Columbia Assessment Authority and Municipal Finance Authority
purposes and not otherwise provide for, as amended by Council, amount to
$2,392,516,008;

AND WHEREAS the total estimated expenditures as aforesaid is the sum of the
appropriation requests of all departments and boards properly supported by detailed
analysis of those expenditures and listings of the salaried staff;

AND WHEREAS the total estimated revenue of the City for 2025 from sources other
than real property taxes, as amended by Council, amounts to $1,168,266,008;

AND WHEREAS the amount of the General Purposes Tax Levy so required is
$1,224,250,000;

THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT the said estimates of the Director of Finance as
amended by Council, both as to totals and individual items pertaining thereto, are hereby
adopted by Council.

ADOPTED ON CONSENT (Vote No. 11524)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)
